items.title: Soundbrenner Spark: the best music tool for young musicians
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: The Soundbrenner Spark is built to ignite musical passion in the next generation.
items.excerptAfter: Discover the Soundbrenner Spark, a vibrating metronome watch designed for young musicians. It features a built-in tuner, dB meter, and more, making it the perfect tool for kids learning music.
items.seoTitleAfter: Soundbrenner Spark: The Best Music Tool for Young Musicians
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Discover the Soundbrenner Spark - a kid-sized vibrating metronome watch with tuner, dB meter, and smart features. Perfect for young musicians learning rhythm and timing.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the Soundbrenner Spark, a kid-friendly vibrating metronome watch designed to support young musicians with essential tools like a tuner and dB…
items.beforeBody:
Parents and music teachers often tell us how perfect our Soundbrenner wearables would be for children. The only problem? Most models don’t fit smaller wrists.

We’re incredibly proud of what the Soundbrenner Core 2 has brought to over 140,000 musicians worldwide: less ear fatigue, a stronger internal sense of rhythm, and a smart wrist tool that combines a metronome, tuner, dB meter, and smartwatch features.

But we realized something important - these powerful tools need to be accessible to every aspiring musician, especially kids.

Introducing the Soundbrenner Spark

That’s why we created the Soundbrenner Spark: a lightweight, kid-friendly version of the Core 2. It's designed to support and inspire young learners on their musical journey.

What’s New in the Spark?

The Spark is built specifically for smaller wrists. It features:

  • A scaled-down body and shorter strap for a snug, comfortable fit
  • A vibrant sky-blue design kids will love

Unlike the Core 2, the Spark has a fixed module. That means no twisting the face off the strap - perfect for avoiding distractions in class or losing parts.

You might be wondering, “If I can’t detach the module, how do I use the tuner?”

That’s where the Spark’s unique design comes in.

A tuner for every instrument

Most young musicians start out with instruments like piano, violin, or flute - not guitar or bass. So instead of a contact tuner, the Spark features a built-in microphone tuner that works across a wide range of instruments.

You’ll still find the same core features Soundbrenner is known for:

  • Vibrating metronome
  • Interactive touch screen
  • Decibel meter
  • Timer, stopwatch, and alarm
  • Optional smartphone notifications

The Spark remains a powerful 4-in-1 music tool, fine-tuned for younger users.

Why choose the Soundbrenner Spark?

Learning rhythm is one of the biggest challenges for young musicians. Traditional metronomes can be loud, distracting, and frustrating - not just for the learner, but for everyone nearby.

The Spark’s vibrating metronome solves that problem by offering a silent, physical cue for the beat. This tactile feedback helps kids internalize rhythm through multiple senses, creating stronger musical foundations.

A complete music learning tool

Music is more than sound - it’s a physical experience. With the Spark, kids learn to engage their sense of touch and build better awareness of rhythm and timing.

Other benefits include:

  • The built-in tuner encourages consistent, accurate tuning habits
  • The dB meter and alarm raise awareness about hearing safety from an early age
  • The sleek, all-day wearable design makes practice reminders effortless

And while it can pair with a phone for notifications, the Spark works entirely on its own - no smartphone required. That means no extra screen time and no need for parental controls.

Spark a lifetime of music

Whether your child is just picking up an instrument or already taking lessons, the Soundbrenner Spark is the perfect companion for their musical journey.

Give them the tools they need to practice smarter, play better, and enjoy every beat of the way.

Soundbrenner Spark is releasing later this year.

items.title: How to play the 12-bar blues chords progression
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving factual content.
items.excerptBefore: The 12-bar blues chords is the most common blues progression and can be heard on countless legendary blues tracks. Check out our playlist!
items.excerptAfter: Learn how to play the 12-bar blues chord progression, a fundamental structure in blues music that allows for creativity and improvisation.
items.seoTitleBefore: How to play the 12-bar blues chords progression
items.seoTitleAfter: How to Play the 12-Bar Blues Chords Progression
items.seoDescriptionBefore: The 12-bar blues chords is the most common blues progression and can be heard on countless legendary blues tracks. Check out our playlist!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the structure of the 12-bar blues chords progression and how to use it in your music. Perfect for improvisation and playing popular blues songs.
items.beforeBody:
Although blues music relies heavily on improvisation, it nevertheless follows some basic structure. This allows musicians to explore their creativity while still being able to play together in a cohesive and controlled manner. Today we will look at the 12-bar blues chords forms and how you can use it.

12-bar blues progression is the most common blues form and can be heard on countless legendary blues tracks. (Check out our playlist below!) When looking at 12-bar blues, we can divide it into 3 groups of 4 bars (hence the name). 

Most 12-bar blues chords are played in 4/4 time. 

Let’s break down what each of these 3 sections consists of:

Blues chords progression

Section 1

Four bars of the…

  • I chord

Total: 4 bars

Section 2

Two bars of the…

  • IV chord
  • I chord

Total: 4 bars

Section 3

One bar of the…

  • V chord
  • IV chord
  • I chord
  • V chord

Total: 4 bars

As a general rule, the third and final section of the 12-bar blues chords is generally used to resolve the first two sections.

Let’s look at an example in the key of A, a common key for blues guitar:

Section 1

Four bars of the…

  • A chord

Total: 4 bars

Section 2

Two bars of the…

  • D chord
  • A chord

Total: 4 bars

Section 3

One bar of the…

  • E chord
  • D chord
  • A chord
  • E chord

Total: 4 bars

From section 3, you would go right back into section 1. This pattern repeats on and on for the entirety of the song.

Using this common structure, you can play a wide variety of popular blues songs, and even improvise over these chords using the pentatonic scale. Try it out today!

If you enjoyed this article, you might want to check out "Effective guitar practice tips"

items.title: 5 tips to start learning to play the bass guitar
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: Do you want to learn how to play the bass guitar but don't know where to start? Here are our top 5 tips to help you learn this new instrument
items.excerptAfter: Discover essential tips for beginners learning to play the bass guitar, including choosing the right instrument, establishing a practice routine, and the importance of feedback.
items.seoTitleBefore: 5 tips to start learning to play the bass guitar
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Tips to Start Learning to Play the Bass Guitar
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Want to learn how to play the bass guitar but don't know where to start? Here are our top 5 tips to help you learn this new instrument
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore five essential tips for beginners looking to learn the bass guitar, from selecting the right instrument to practicing effectively and playing with…
items.beforeBody:
Learning how to play the bass guitar can be daunting. Four strings instead of six strings? It may seem as if it's a whole new language to learn.

Playing bass guitar can sometimes be seen as the least important instrument in the band, as bassists normally stand right at the back of the stage, close to the drummer. In reality, they provide the groove of a song, and they are the reason you bob your head to a rhythm.

1. Get a bass

Might sound obvious, but the right place to start. Nowadays, there are multiple choices for basses that won't break the bank. Brand-new instruments are affordable, but a second-hand bass is also a good option. Essentially, there are two main types of basses: the precision bass and the jazz bass.

So which of these should you buy? A general rule of thumb is to look at the kinds of basses played by the people who inspire you.

Is it Flea from the RHCP? In that case, the best option could be a MusicMan or a Fender Jazz Bass.

Don't choose based on looks alone — you have to like how it sounds, so try as many basses as possible within your budget.

Also, — split your account between your bass and your amp. The amp is just as important as the bass. Some may argue that it is more essential; for instance, it could be better to have an expensive amp with a cheap bass than the other way around.

2. Have a practice routine

At beginner stages, your aim should be to practice at least four times a week, even if for five minutes; the objective is to show up, to do your reps. It's better to practice four times a week for 5 minutes than 20 minutes within a day because you are trying to build a habit.

Ideally, practice at the same time every day; I practice almost first thing in the morning for 30 minutes. No matter how the day goes, I have a clear conscience that I've already played my instrument.

Devote a space at home for your music practice, and never put your instrument in its case.

Devote a space at home for your music practice, have everything you need ready in advance, and never put your instrument in its case. That way, if you have less time, it is spent practicing and not just preparing your gear.

3. Get feedback

To progress as fast as you can, you need to know if you are doing things correctly. It is crucial to start with good technique — from proper posture, and plucking, to fretting and hand technique. If you skip these fundamentals, you won't have to correct them later.

The best way to get feedback is from a good teacher. You can make adjustments on your own, but an experienced teacher must look at what you are doing in person or by recording yourself and sending the material to your instructor.

Another way to get feedback is from a metronome; time is everything in music and is especially important to rhythmic instruments, such as the bass. You've got to love a metronome.

4. Play with others

This may be the best tip I can offer you. Jamming with a drummer can provide additional feedback; there are no wrong notes!

I recommend playing with people with slightly better skills than you — this will force you to match that level.

It can be intimidating initially, but trust me, it's worth it. When in doubt, use the Nike strategy: "Just do it."

5. Be patient

The bass is a relatively easy instrument to start; as I said earlier, you can and should start playing with other musicians right away. Playing roots and fifths is no big deal, but things become tricky as with any other instrument, so be patient. It takes time to master "the low end."

"May the force be with you and your bass."

We made a Spotify playlist filled with bass-inspiration just for you!

Check it out:

 

This article is contributed by Jorge Rodriguez Sierra, father of two, aspiring pro-musician, bassist, blogger and physician-anesthesiologist.

items.title: Learning drums: A How-to guide for beginners
items.editorNotes: The article has been rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content and structure.
items.excerptBefore: Camellia Akhamie Kies is a professional drummer, percussionist, songwriter, music producer, designer, teacher, and a full-time mom.
items.excerptAfter: A beginner's guide to learning drums, featuring tips from professional drummer Camillia Akhamie Kies.
items.seoTitleBefore: Learning drums: A How-to guide for beginners
items.seoTitleAfter: Learning Drums: A How-to Guide for Beginners
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Learn drums with Camellia Akhamie Kies, a professional drummer, percussionist, music producer, designer, songwriter and full-time mom
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore essential tips and advice for beginners learning to play drums, featuring insights from professional drummer Camillia Akhamie Kies.
items.beforeBody:
Written by: Drummer & full-time mom, Camillia Akhamie Kies

Interested in learning drums? Looking for a place to start?

Our “How to play drums: a guide for beginners (by professional drummers)” is a new series filled with essential tips and advice from musicians who are learning drums.

Our goal is for this to be a series that will inspire you to pick up those drumsticks and start drumming. Drummer and full-time mom, Camillia Akhamie Kies is a passionate drummer, composer, and charismatic influencer. It's our honor to have her present part due of our series. Let’s get started!

Anyone can learn to play drum

We all have a heartbeat, which means we all have rhythm. I hear rhythms all around me, and I have been known to help people find their own unique rhythmic voice on drums and percussion.

Is rhythm innate?

I believe rhythm is something you can be born with. However, mastering rhythm is something anyone can learn with practice.

Never too early to start playing drums.

I played drums my entire pregnancy and after my son was born, I put drumsticks in his hands as soon as he could hold them. He was about 5 months old when he could actually hold the sticks on his own, prior to that I was helping him hold the sticks and showing him how to move his wrist to play the drums.

Avoid drumming with bad technique

It is important to spend time learning how to properly hold the sticks. It helps prevent injuries and bad habits that may take a long time to relearn. When I first started to learn, I started with traditional griping. However, I learned it incorrectly, and It took me lots of practice and a long time to relearn it.

I wanted to play snare in the drum line and having the incorrect technique did not work because everything is supposed to be uniform.

How often should I practice?

Today I practice about 2 hours every day, however, during high school, I practiced about 4–6 hours every day. During my summer breaks from school, I would practice about 8 hours a day. Meal breaks were not included at that time.

Practicing could mean listening to music, working on sight-reading notation, and of course, drumming.

Good songs to start playing drums with

If you are brand new to drums, I recommend learning the below.

  • “We will Rock You” by Queen
  • Beat It, by Michael Jackson.

Both are great songs that utilize quarter notes and eight notes.

If you are a little more advanced, I would recommend Taylor Swift's “Shake It Off”. It's a great pick to work on syncopated rhythms.

Should drummers learn melodic instruments?

Yes!!! Drummers should definitely learn melodic instruments. Learning the xylophone/marimba will help you understand the piano. Whilst, learning about the bass guitar will help you interact and communicate better musically when playing with other musicians.

Mobile apps for drummers

There are a few different recommendations on this, and getting the Score Creator App is undoubtedly one of them. It's perfect for writing easy drum charts and listening back to the notation you created.

Can also recommend getting a Piano or a Xylophone app to work on writing melodies and thinking more melodically.

Lastly, of course, the Soundbrenner app. It has to be at the top of my list because it has the best breakdown of rhythms. Many of my students also have selected Soundbrenner as their metronome app of choice.

My last recommendation is to get an app called “Acapella”. It's perfect for making quick side-by-side videos with other musicians and creators on social media.

How do I protect my hearing?

I protect my hearing by wearing in-ear monitors. The in-ears help me block out some sounds of my drums. It also allows me to create a nice mix without having anything too loud. I also check out how loud my music is by using the decibel meter on the Soundbrenner Core Steel! It's a great tool to utilize to prevent severe hearing damage.

Camellia Akhamie Kies is a professional drummer, Percussionist, songwriter, music producer, designer, teacher, and Full-time-mom.

Over the last decade, Camellia has traveled to over 20 countries sharing her love and passion for music. She began her music career at age 19 after joining the United States Navy Music Program.

She spent the majority of her military service in Japan traveling South East Asia, teaching at schools, and performing at concerts, festivals, and military events.

After being honorably discharged from the military in 2014, she attended University to work on her own unique sound.

items.excerptBefore: Are you looking for a high-quality Smartphone Studio Mic? Introducing the newest product in our shop, a must-have for any musicians.

items.seoTitleBefore: Smartphone Studio Mic: high-quality audio recording on any device
items.seoTitleAfter: Smartphone Studio Mic: High-Quality Audio Recording Anywhere
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Are you looking for a high-quality Smartphone Studio Mic? Introducing the newest product in our shop, a must-have for any musicians.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the Smartphone Studio Mic, a versatile and portable microphone for musicians and content creators. Record studio-quality audio on your smartphone…
items.beforeBody:
If you’re a serious musician, you might already have some sort of microphone setup at home. But studio setups can be unnecessarily complicated and cumbersome. For example, what happens if you want to record something on the go? It isn’t always feasible to carry your computer, audio interface, XLR cables, and studio mics with you. What if you’re an aspiring content creator who wants to record for Instagram or TikTok? It’s inconvenient to record video and audio separately and then sync them together with editing software. Especially if you’re more of a casual musician, you might just want a simple setup that’s easy to use. This is where the Smartphone Studio Mic comes in. Whether you’re a professional or a hobbyist, this mic lets you create the recordings you need. Without sacrificing quality or convenience — you'll be able to record everything with convenience.

Convenience

We believe that the best music tools are the ones you can take with you wherever you go. That is why we are launching our very first third-party microphone product, the Smartphone Studio Mic. This microphone is all about portable use; doesn't even require an audio interface or even a computer. All you need is a smartphone, and you’re good to go. The mic plugs directly into your phone’s USB-C or Lightning port. You don't have to use software or a driver. Your smartphone will automatically detect the mic and use it for all audio recordings. You also do not need to charge the mic; as your phone will directly power it. This makes it the perfect microphone for convenient portable recording.

Compatibility

The Smartphone Studio Mic is universally compatible and works with both iOS and Android phones, as well as tablets. If you want, you can even use it with your Windows or Mac computer. To ensure that all your devices are supported, we have included 3 different cables: USB-C, Lightning, and USB-A. Even better, the mic comes with a carrying pouch, mini tripod, and microphone mount. You won’t have to spend a single cent on additional accessories.

Just like its name suggests, the Smartphone Studio Mic enables studio-quality recordings on your mobile device. With a frequency response of 20Hz -20kHz, this mic is capable of capturing the full audio spectrum of your instrument or vocals. The 85dB signal-to-noise ratio allows it to be silent even when recording quieter sound sources, and the cardioid polar pickup pattern ensures that it captures sound directly in front while effectively reducing background noise. This is a big upgrade compared to built-in smartphone mics, which tend to be noisy and capture everything, including unwanted sounds.

The Smartphone Studio Mic also features additional functionality such as integrated gain control and a headphone output for direct monitoring. This means you can adjust your recording volume directly on the mic itself, and also listen to your recording in real-time.

The Smartphone Studio Mic opens up an endless list of possibilities. You could use it as a portable live-streaming device to upgrade the audio quality of your Instagram Live videos. Record yourself playing guitar and upload directly to YouTube without needing to splice the video and audio together separately. You could take it with you on tour to capture any song ideas you have without needing a studio setup. You could even keep it on your desk with the included tripod and use it as your daily Zoom meeting mic.

With all these features at an affordable price point, the Smartphone Studio Mic is perfect for anyone who needs to record audio, regardless of the application. Click here to order yours now!

BUY NOW

This is especially true for aspiring content creators who want to record for platforms like Instagram or TikTok. Recording video and audio separately and then syncing them in editing software can be inconvenient. If you’re a casual musician, you might just want a simple setup that’s easy to use. This is where the Smartphone Studio Mic comes in. Whether you’re a professional or a hobbyist, this mic allows you to create the recordings you need without sacrificing quality or convenience.

Convenience

We believe that the best music tools are those you can take with you wherever you go. That is why we are launching our very first third-party microphone product, the Smartphone Studio Mic. This microphone is designed for portable use and does not require an audio interface or a computer. All you need is a smartphone, and you’re ready to go. The mic plugs directly into your phone’s USB-C or Lightning port. There’s no need for software or drivers; your smartphone will automatically detect the mic for all audio recordings. Additionally, the mic is powered directly by your phone, eliminating the need for charging. This makes it the perfect microphone for convenient portable recording.

Compatibility

The Smartphone Studio Mic is universally compatible, working with both iOS and Android phones, as well as tablets. You can even use it with your Windows or Mac computer. To ensure compatibility with all your devices, we have included three different cables: USB-C, Lightning, and USB-A. The mic also comes with a carrying pouch, mini tripod, and microphone mount, so you won’t need to spend extra on additional accessories.

As its name suggests, the Smartphone Studio Mic enables studio-quality recordings on your mobile device. With a frequency response of 20Hz to 20kHz, this mic captures the full audio spectrum of your instrument or vocals. The 85dB signal-to-noise ratio allows it to remain silent even when recording quieter sound sources. The cardioid polar pickup pattern captures sound directly in front while effectively reducing background noise, making it a significant upgrade compared to built-in smartphone mics, which often capture unwanted sounds.

The Smartphone Studio Mic also features integrated gain control and a headphone output for direct monitoring. This allows you to adjust your recording volume directly on the mic and listen to your recording in real-time.

The Smartphone Studio Mic opens up endless possibilities. You could use it as a portable live-streaming device to enhance the audio quality of your Instagram Live videos. Record yourself playing guitar and upload directly to YouTube without needing to splice the video and audio together separately. Take it with you on tour to capture song ideas without needing a studio setup. You could even keep it on your desk with the included tripod and use it as your daily Zoom meeting mic.

With all these features at an affordable price point, the Smartphone Studio Mic is perfect for anyone who needs to record audio, regardless of the application. Click here to order yours now!

BUY NOW

items.title: Metronome practice as a drummer: 5 tips to improve
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining original content.
items.excerptBefore: Improving metronome practice as a drummer can be difficult in the beginning, which is why we've listed our top 5 tips to help you.
items.excerptAfter: Discover five effective tips to enhance your metronome practice as a drummer, from drilling weak spots to practicing fills.
items.seoTitleBefore: Metronome practice as a drummer: 5 tips to improve.
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Tips to Improve Metronome Practice as a Drummer
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Improving metronome practice as a drummer can be difficult in the beginning, which is why we've listed our top 5 tips to help you.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Enhance your drumming skills with these five effective metronome practice tips. Learn to identify weak spots, practice at different tempos, and more.
items.beforeBody:
Improving metronome practice as a drummer can be hard, so that’s why we have listed five tips. Get started with downloading The Metronome and get on with these tips.

1. Drill the weak stuff

Practice areas where you find the most trouble, those bars where you derail from the usual groove, and boom, you jump back in a second too late.

Improving metronome practice as a drummer requires obviously the use of a metronome. This way, you'll have a standard way to find your weak spots and perfect the areas that you find yourself having problems with. Loop three bars of groove and a bar of whatever it is you’re finding it hard to stay in time with. You’ll probably find yourself pushing or pulling depending on what you’re playing, but the more you play it, the tighter you’ll get!

2. Start slow to play fast

The most obvious use of a metronome is to help drummers slow down difficult passages while keeping the pace consistent. Metronome practice as a drummer lets you work through challenging bars without making mistakes, and lets you gradually increase the speed as you become more familiar with it.

One effective technique that lets you build up speed is to find a challenging section and isolate it when practicing. Identify the shortest note value in the section. Set your metronome to 60 BPM and practice through the passage with the shortest note value equal to 60 BPM.

For example, if the shortest note value is a sixteenth note then practice the passage so that every sixteenth note receives one beat, every eighth note receives two beats, etc… Once you’ve done this flawlessly, increase the metronome speed till you get as fast as you want to go!

3. Practice at different tempos

Vary the speed of the click so that you are able to play beats and fills at ALL tempos. Some drummers find that certain tempos are more difficult to play along to than others, so work at these speeds the most. Metronome practice as a drummer helps you identify which tempos challenge you the most. Try the metronome at different tempos and see where your weak spots are and really work on them.

4. Displace the first beat

Who says the click of the metronome has to be the downbeat? If you’re swinging or playing reggae, you can do amazing things for your sound by playing off the beat! Just as long as you can resist not making your beat 1 the metronomes beat 1. You can go wild and just displace by a 1/16th note, have fun with it, really get into the groove and swing of things!

5. Practice practice practice your fills

Speeding up or slowing down during drum fills is something that happens to every drummer. It's really common to rush fills during slower tempos or speed up during fast tempos. When practicing fills with a metronome, make sure you’re keeping them in the context of the groove. Metronome practice as a drummer helps you stay consistent when working on fills.  Practice in groups of four measures, with three bars of groove and a bar of fill, or two bars of groove and a bar of fill.
items.afterBody:
Improving metronome practice as a drummer can be challenging. Here are five tips to help you enhance your skills. Start by downloading The Metronome and follow these tips.

1. Drill the weak stuff

Focus on the areas where you struggle the most, especially the bars where you lose the groove and fall behind. Using a metronome will help you identify your weak spots and improve those challenging sections. Loop three bars of groove followed by a bar of the difficult passage. You may find yourself pushing or pulling, but with practice, you'll tighten up your timing.

2. Start slow to play fast

The primary function of a metronome is to help drummers slow down difficult passages while maintaining a consistent pace. This allows you to work through challenging bars without making mistakes and gradually increase the speed as you become more comfortable.

To build speed effectively, isolate a challenging section. Identify the shortest note value in that section and set your metronome to 60 BPM. Practice the passage so that each sixteenth note receives one beat, each eighth note receives two beats, and so on. Once you can play it flawlessly, gradually increase the metronome speed until you reach your desired tempo.

3. Practice at different tempos

Vary the metronome speed to play beats and fills at all tempos. Some drummers find specific tempos more challenging, so focus on those. Metronome practice will help you identify your weak spots across different tempos, allowing you to work on them effectively.

4. Displace the first beat

Who says the metronome click has to be the downbeat? When playing styles like swing or reggae, you can enhance your sound by playing off the beat. Just ensure that your beat 1 does not align with the metronome's beat 1. Try displacing by a sixteenth note and enjoy the groove!

5. Practice your fills

Speeding up or slowing down during drum fills is common among drummers. It's essential to maintain consistency while practicing fills with a metronome. Keep your fills in the context of the groove by practicing in groups of four measures: three bars of groove followed by a bar of fill, or two bars of groove and a bar of fill.

items.title: A year-end letter from Soundbrenner 2022
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original message and factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Taking a look back at 2022 with Soundbrenner with a year-end letter from the founder and CEO of Soundbrenner
items.excerptAfter: Reflecting on 2022, Soundbrenner celebrates significant milestones and new features for musicians, while looking forward to an even better 2023.
items.seoTitleBefore: A year-end letter from Soundbrenner 2022
items.seoTitleAfter: Soundbrenner Year-End Letter 2022
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Taking a look back at 2022 with Soundbrenner with a year-end letter from the founder and CEO of Soundbrenner
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover Soundbrenner's achievements in 2022, including new features for musicians and exciting hardware releases. Join us in looking forward to 2023!
items.beforeBody:
Taking a look back at 2022...

This year, our app and wearables were used in over 12 million music sessions to practice over 5 million hours of music. Converted into years, that's over 600 years worth of music that was made with our products! 🥰

When we started Soundbrenner a few years ago, we could only dream about bringing so much musicality to the world. Making music is a superpower, and helping so many people to obtain and refine this superpower is the opportunity of my lifetime! We can't thank you all enough.

What we shipped in 2022 for musicians

Most of the features below were suggestions made by musicians just like you. Go to suggestions.soundbrenner.com and submit your ideas and upvote what we should build next!

Here are just a couple of excerpts of what's new!

iOS

This year, we wanted to help you make it easier to build a practice habit and make each practice session more rewarding:

 

Practice reminders: In Settings → App settings → App notifications, you now have the option to create recurring practice reminders and set up motivational messages that hold you accountable!

Practice tracking: Keep count of how many hours you practice each week and set practice goals. Can you keep your practice streak going?

Brag with your musical accomplishments: Automatically generate a shareable image of your latest music session or your current practice streak.

 

Android

This year, we were finally able to rebuild our in-house Android team. This allowed us to give our Android users some well-deserved love and catch up with iOS!

Metronome library updates: Song and set list sharing! You can now even export and import cross-platform between iOS & Android devices.

Soundbrenner Learn: Master your rhythm with videos made by world-class instructors.

New color scheme and many polishes: We think details matter and this belief is finally starting to show in our Android app too. Expect many more improvements to come next year!

 

Both iOS & Android:

New modifier menu in metronome: Set up incremental tempo changes (accelerando!), train with muted beats and more!

New hardware

Genki Wave: We partnered with our friends from Genki to bring their Wave MIDI ring to soundbrenner.com. It allows you to add sound effects via hand gestures. It will open up a whole world of creativity. Check it out here!

2022 Core Limited Edition: We dropped a highly limited edition of our bestselling Core in space cadet blue, with special engravings and many extra goodies. Sadly it's already sold out but stay on the lookout for more to come in 2023!

Smartphone studio mic: We are all about removing friction and removing barriers to your musicality. With this new microphone we launched in 2022, you can record high-quality audio on any device, including your smartphone. It's perfect to create footage to upload on social media! Learn more about it here!

Outlook

2022 was awesome, but we will work hard to make 2023 even better. What would you like to see next? Don't forget to send us your ideas via suggestions.soundbrenner.com or email me via florian@soundbrenner.com. 

Sincerely,
Florian
Founder & CEO of Soundbrenner

items.title: Celebrating women in music
items.editorNotes: Ensure that all links are functional and images are properly displayed.
items.excerptBefore: This article will pay homage to the brilliant women who have left an indelible mark on the music industry, celebrating women in music!
items.excerptAfter: This article celebrates the contributions of prominent women in music history, highlighting their impact and legacy in the industry.
items.seoTitleAfter: Celebrating Women in Music
items.seoDescriptionBefore: This article will pay homage to the brilliant women who have left an indelible mark on the music industry, celebrating women in music!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the significant contributions of iconic female musicians in the music industry and their lasting impact on future generations.
items.beforeBody:
Women remain significantly underrepresented in the music industry, with a 10-year study by Dr. Stacy L. Smith and her team at the USC Annenberg Inclusion Initiative finding that 97.2% of music producers are male and only 2.8% are female. Therefore, we must continue celebrating women who have made history in the field of music.

This article will focus on some of the most prominent females in the industry in the post-20th century era; however, we must not forget the impacts of women of the centuries lost in history. Although their influence is much lost to time, often uncredited and ignored, their impact on the music industry is invaluable.

Consequently, we must remember their accomplishments and recognize their importance in the history of music. So that their legacy lives on and can inspire future generations of female musicians.


1. Aretha Franklin

One of the most iconic vocalists of the 20th century, Aretha Franklin has rightfully been dubbed the Queen of Soul. Through her over 100 singles placed on the US Billboard Charts, she has left her mark on many musicians forever. Additionally, Franklin is a top selection of Rolling Stone's "100 Greatest Singers of All Time" and has earned 44 Grammy Awards — a true testament to her accomplishment.

In the words of President Obama, "Nobody embodies more fully the connection between the African American spiritual, the blues, R&B, rock and roll—the way that hardship and sorrow were transformed into something full of beauty and vitality and hope."


2. Beyoncé

Beyoncé is the most-awarded artist at the Grammys, with 32 awards and immense cultural significance in 21st-century pop music.

Furthermore, she is one of the world's best-selling recording artists alongside Whitney Houston, Celine Dion, Madonna, Mariah Carey, and Taylor Swift, having sold over 200 million records worldwide. Additionally, Beyoncé's impact on 21st-century pop music and the industry is immense and empowering for young black women and artists.


3. Patti Smith

Often dubbed the "Punk Poet Laureate", Patti Smith's fusion of poetry and rock music made her a prominent figure in the NYC punk rock movement. Despite few mainstream awards, she was 47th on Rolling Stone's "100 Greatest Artists of All Time". Madonna, Michael Stipe of R.E.M. and Courtney Love have credited Smith as a major influence. Smith further makes her mark on literature, receiving the National Book Award for her 2010 memoir "Just Kids". She also gained the PEN America Literary Service Award in 2020.


4. Whitney Houston

Whitney Houston is iconic in the music industry, holding the Guinness World Record for being the Highest Awarded Female Artist of all time. She has achieved over 400 awards, including Album of the Year at the 28th Grammy Awards and Favorite Soul/R&B Album and Artist at the 27th American Music Awards.

Music Critic Andy Gill wrote, "...Whitney, more than any other single artist – Michael Jackson included – effectively mapped out the course of modern R&B, setting the bar for standards of soul vocalese and creating the original template for what we now routinely address as the 'soul diva'." And we couldn't agree more-her influence and prowess have landed her a well-deserved spot in music history.


5. Sister Rosetta Tharpe

We couldn't leave out the incomparable Sister Rosetta Tharpe, widely known as the "Godmother of rock and roll". She is truly iconic for numerous reasons, including her flamboyancy in swing and blues and her role in pushing spiritual music into the mainstream. As a result, we have Sister Rosetta Tharpe to thank for the rise of the pop gospel.

Tharpe's collaboration "Strange Things Happening Every Day" with jazz and blues musician, Sammy Price, became the first gospel record to hit no.2 on Billboard's "race records" chart. Tharpe never lived to receive numerous awards. However, her cultural significance is irreplaceable. The path she's forged for crossovers between gospel and swing has made her a necessary figure in music history.

This list does not do justice to the countless incredible female musicians who have been the powerful voices of those who remain unheard. We must continue celebrating these women in music for now and forever!

Thanks for reading our article "Celebrating Women in Music." If you enjoyed this one, you can check out our article "Celebrating LGBTQ+ Music Artists in 2025."

items.title: Finding your tempo without a metronome or tap tool
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original meaning and factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Don't have a metronome on hand? Learn to find tempo without a metronome with our BPM cheat sheet and train up your internal clock.
items.excerptAfter: Learn how to find your tempo without a metronome using familiar songs as references.
items.seoTitleBefore: Finding your tempo without a metronome or tap tool
items.seoTitleAfter: Finding Your Tempo Without a Metronome
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Don't have a metronome on hand? Learn to find tempo without a metronome with our BPM cheat sheet and train up your internal clock.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover how to find your tempo without a metronome using familiar songs as BPM references. Check out our cheat sheet for song tempos!
items.beforeBody:
Did you know that finding your tempo without a metronome is possible? While It may seem impossible at first, with our BPM cheat sheet, you might be surprised by how well you know some tempos already!  

Let’s say your phone just died, and you find yourself stuck without a metronome. (This is assuming you already have a metronome app downloaded on your phone — if not, check out ours here.) You have to play something at 118 BPM. Given most of us don’t have absolute tempo, how on earth do you find your tempo without a metronome?

Don’t panic. If you were asked to sing the chorus of Don’t Stop Believin’ or Bad Romance, you could probably do that, right?

If so, good news! These songs are at 119 BPM. If you can sing either of these songs, you can recall their pulse, which means you have an internal clock. This means you can use the songs you know as BPM references.  

 

Okay, so you probably won't get a perfect 119 BPM on your first go, because we're human and not perfect metronomes. However, you'll definitely be closer with a reference point than if you tried to lift your tempo out of thin air.

Additionally, we've put together a handy cheat sheet of songs (and tempo markings) for you below. Therefore, for whatever BPM you need, you can recall a corresponding song and feel its groove. Internalize that beat, and you've found your tempo without a metronome! 

With time, you'll train up your tempo memorization and become the best possible human metronome. Test out your internal clock with the tap feature on our app or watch, and see which songs give you the best accuracy for tempo identification!

Your BPM cheat sheet to finding your tempo without a metronome

Once you learn to identify BPMs through specific songs and grooves, it’ll become a breeze to find tempo without a metronome! We hope this helps you find your tempo without a metronome – or, never go without one by wearing it on you! 

Check out The Metronome App by Soundbrenner

Your BPM cheat sheet to finding your tempo without a metronome

50 BPM*

*It’s harder to grasp a precise tempo with slower songs. We recommend finding the
tempo that’s twice as fast and treating every other
beat as a subdivision.

items.title: Closer look at the Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse
items.editorNotes: Ensure that all links are functional and images are properly displayed.
items.excerptBefore: We will be taking a closer look at Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse in this article. Finally our wearables family will be complete!
items.excerptAfter: Explore the features of the Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse, our innovative wearable devices designed for musicians.
items.seoTitleBefore: Closer look at the Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse
items.seoTitleAfter: Closer Look at the Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse
items.seoDescriptionBefore: We will be taking a closer look at Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse in this article. Finally our wearables family will be complete!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the features and innovations of the Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse, our latest wearable devices for musicians.
items.beforeBody:
We're excited about our big Kickstarter launch! Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse, our wearable family, will finally be complete. Until then, we’ll try to feed you as much information as we possibly can. Today’s article is about taking a look at our entire family of great wearables.

Product features: Core 2

Our two new products, The Soundbrenner Core 2 and Soundbrenner Core 2 Steel, will add an entirely new layer of functions in comparison to the Soundbrenner Pulse. The Core 2 series will, like the Pulse, feature the world’s best smart vibrating metronome while adding even more tools, like the Magnetic Twist Tuner, a decibel meter, and your everyday watch. Both the Core 2 and Core 2 Steel have a distinctive look but carry an identical set of tools and features. So no matter which one you go for, you’ll always be ready to make it count.

The Core 2 is made from super durable polycarbonate and aluminum, while the Core 2 Steel is made from premium stainless steel. The two new wearables have decreased in size but still deliver the same, powerful vibrations. We've taken Soundbrenner Pulse 7G ERM motor and added it to our new line.

Innovation with the addition of Pulse

So, our family is complete. The Soundbrenner Pulse will continue to deliver on being the world’s best smart vibrating metronome. Moreover, our new Core 2 series will give musicians something totally new and fresh. We’ll continue to push the limits and make sure that you have the tools to help you master your craft.

Want to learn more about the different tools? Read more about the Soundbrenner Core 2 here.

Don’t forget that we are launching soon! If you haven’t already, join our exclusive Facebook group where you’ll get even more photos and information about our launch.

items.title: RJ Ronquillo | Life lessons from a professional musician
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content's integrity.
items.excerptBefore: RJ Ronquillo, a professional musician, shares some tips on the industry. He also tells us his story and the shift in his career.
items.excerptAfter: RJ Ronquillo shares his journey as a professional musician, offering insights on motivation, challenges, and advice for aspiring artists.
items.seoTitleBefore: RJ Ronquillo | Life lessons from a professional musician
items.seoTitleAfter: Life Lessons from Professional Musician RJ Ronquillo
items.seoDescriptionBefore: RJ Ronquillo, a professional musician, shares some tips on the industry. He also tells us his story and the shift in his career.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover RJ Ronquillo's inspiring journey in the music industry, his motivations, and valuable advice for aspiring musicians.
items.beforeBody:
RJ shares some inspiring tips to become a professional musician. He also shares his experience entering the music industry and why he's chosen to take a break to focus on creating content on YouTube.

Make Music Better is a series of intimate conversations with professional musicians who know what it takes to master their craft.

Growing up as an 80s kid, RJ Ronquillo was exposed to a lot of MTV, movies, and radio. Pop culture at an early age channelled his passion to be on stage and perform.

After finishing university with a degree in studio music and jazz, RJ slowly but surely got to performing at small gigs. It was a roller coaster ride from getting random day jobs to finally becoming a professional musician. Eventually, he performed at international arenas with famous artists like Judith Hill and Ricky Martin.

RJ has recently decided to put his touring career on hold. This gave him time to make creative YouTube videos promoting different music brands and educating students with technical skills in music. We interviewed RJ on what his day-to-day looks like.

How long have you been a professional musician, and what do you love about it?

"Music is one of the few things that I've never gotten bored of. For this whole time that I've been a musician, I always learn new things about the guitar, and it never bores me. It's always been fascinating to me. When I'm playing with other musicians, and it's grooving, and it's right, there's a spark - when everything clicks; it just feels fantastic."

“RJ: As far as the music that I love playing, everything almost blues rock n' roll, country, funk fusion, RnB, flamenco, classical, you name it, I love it all.”

Who or what inspired you to pursue a career in music?

The movies that I saw. Back to the Future, Labamba, those are probably one of the single most important things that made me want to play the guitar. I'll admit when I was a kid, I wanted to be famous, and I wanted to be on stage. It was the pop culture at the time of the 80s that pushed me in that direction.

Knowing the challenges of being a professional musician, why did you gravitate towards music as your career?

I think it was just something I knew I could do. Feeling that I have a chance, was passionate enough, and had some talent there. I just wanted to go for it. How was I ever to find out unless I tried, so I just jumped into the pool and hoped for the best.

“RJ: It’s something that can get us out of the real world aHow was I ever to find out unless i tried, so I just jumped into the pool and hoped for the best.”

Can you describe what you do?

A typical day for me now has changed a bit, so I used to be a professional touring musician for 18 years. But recently, with the advent of social media and the internet, I've chosen to take a break from touring and found a way to be creative by making YouTube videos and teaching through YouTube and Instagram and promoting gear and products.

How do you stay motivated?

If you are always on social media as much as I am on YouTube, there are constantly great musicians out there that are putting out their videos and their content, and everyone is inspiring to me. Seeing the younger generation play fantastic music, being super innovative and creative. This is what pushes me to keep on practicing and being creative myself.

What was one of your proudest moments?

One of my proudest moments was my first big major production tour with Ricky Martin, who is a famous Latin singer, and this was back in 2007. We did a world tour, and this was mostly arena stadiums. I was super excited and happy to do that. My parents saw me playing in an arena in Orlando, and I remember them playing and seeing them in the crowd, and they were smiling and cheering me on. Seeing them see me made me feel proud, as they knew that I could be a successful musician.

“RJ: My parents saw me playing in an arena in Orlando, and they were cheering... To see them see me made me feel proud.”

In your opinion, what is the most important quality a person needs to have to be a successful musician?

Personality. We say in the professional music industry that your talent might get you the gig, but it's your personality that is going to keep the gig. If you're on tour, you need to be someone who is a pleasure to be around. It's essential to have a positive attitude toward things. In short, talent is a big part of it, but human interaction is necessary. Music is all about playing with other musicians; it's very much a communal thing.

“RJ: ...your talent might get you the gig, but it's your personality that is going to keep the gig."

What advice would you give to other musicians who are just starting?

Just go for it and don't pay attention to what other people are doing. Don't compare yourself to other musicians. Don't say I'm not good enough. Just do what you do and put all your heart and soul into it. Be passionate about it. Therefore, if you are not passionate about it, then it's not for you. That goes with any profession; you have to love what you are doing genuinely.

RJ Ronquillo wears the Soundbrenner Core 2 in his interview video. The Soundbrenner Core 2 is a multipurpose watch for musicians, check out more features here.
